description: Interactive torch-and-material stage that teaches transparent, translucent, and opaque materials. A torch on the left shines light to the right. A 3-position slider in the middle swaps between clear glass, butter paper, and a wooden board. A friend silhouette on the right shows the resulting view: clear, fuzzy, or blacked out. A live caption names the classification, and a 3-card rule strip keeps the definitions visible.
ncertChapter: Curiosity Class 6, Chapter 6: Materials Around Us, Section 6.3.3
learningObjectives: Identify materials as transparent, translucent, or opaque
Predict what a viewer can see through different materials
Connect everyday objects (glass, butter paper, wood) to their optical classification

width.description: Component max width in pixels (responsive — will shrink to container)

height.description: Minimum component height in pixels (auto on mobile)

showModeSelector.description: Show or hide the mode-selector tab bar

enabledModes.description: Which modes are visible in the selector

filterSteps.description: Only show these step IDs (filters within current mode)

autoPlayDuration.description: Auto-advance delay in ms. Set to 0 to disable auto-advance.

themeColor.description: Primary brand color (purple from design system)

darkMode.description: Dark mode toggle (light mode follows design PDF tokens)

initialMaterial.description: Which material to display first when the tool loads

availableMaterials.description: Restrict the slider to a subset of materials (e.g. only show 2 for a comparison question)

showRuleCard.description: Show the bottom 3-card rule strip with definitions

showLightBeam.description: Render the animated light cone from the torch

showCaption.description: Show the live classification caption (transparent / translucent / opaque)

autoCycle.description: Automatically cycle through available materials on a timer (good for hands-off demos)

cycleInterval.description: Milliseconds between auto-cycle material swaps (only when autoCycle is true)

studentName.description: Optional student name for personalized captions (not used in default copy)

examples.name: Direct answer — what does opaque mean?

learn.label: Learn
learn.description: Guided walkthrough: torch intro, then transparent, translucent, opaque in sequence

practice.label: Practice
practice.description: Free interaction with the slider; user predicts before checking caption

real_world.label: Real World
real_world.description: Connect classifications to everyday objects (windows, frosted glass, walls)

hands_on.label: Hands On
hands_on.description: Self-paced exploration with full slider control

responsive.mobile: < 640px — stacked vertical layout, smaller controls, single-column rule card
responsive.desktop: ≥ 900px — full horizontal stage with all elements visible

whenToUse: Student asks what transparent / translucent / opaque mean
Class 6 science lesson on materials and how light passes through them
Student needs to classify an everyday object (window, butter paper, door)
Comparison question between two material types

introduce_concept.instructions_for_student: Slide the switch to change the material. Watch what the friend sees and read the label.

show_one_material_only.instructions_for_student: Butter paper is translucent — some light gets through but the image is fuzzy.

free_exploration.instructions_for_student: Try all three materials. What changes about your view?
